15: The temple complex in Jerusalem (Herod's temple, begun 20 BCE, not completed until 63 CE — just 7 years before its destruction) was enormous: the Temple Mount platform covered approximately 36 acres. The Court of the Gentiles (the outermost court, open to non-Jews) was where the money-changers and animal sellers operated. The Tyrian shekel was required for the half-shekel temple tax because its silver purity was reliable; pilgrims coming from across the diaspora needed to exchange their local currency. The commerce occupied the only space designated for Gentile prayer, effectively excluding the nations from the one place in the temple complex that was theirs. Mark's preservation of for all nations (Isa 56:7) makes the exclusion explicit.
1: Bethphage ("house of unripe figs") was a village near the summit of the Mount of Olives, east of Jerusalem; Bethany ("house of dates" or "house of the poor") was on the eastern slopes of the Mount of Olives, about 3km from Jerusalem — the village of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus (John 11). The Mount of Olives (754m) rises east of Jerusalem across the Kidron valley, with a clear view of the temple complex. Zechariah 14:4 predicted that "the LORD will stand on the Mount of Olives" in the day of judgment — the approach from the east via the Mount of Olives carried eschatological freight for Jewish pilgrims who knew the prophecy.
2: The village ahead is identified in Luke 19:29 as Bethphage. A colt (pōlos — a young horse or donkey, in this context a donkey) that "no one has ever ridden" would be ritually appropriate for a sacred purpose: in the OT, animals used for holy purposes were required to be ones that had never been yoked or worked (Num 19:2; Deut 21:3; 1 Sam 6:7). The instruction to untie it and bring it suggests a prior arrangement: the phrase "the Lord needs it and will send it back shortly" functions as a code or password established in advance.
3: "The Lord needs it" (ho kyrios autou chreian echei) — the phrasing is ambiguous: "the Lord" could refer to Jesus (using a Christological title) or to the colt's owner (in the sense of "its master needs it back"). Either reading is coherent in context. The arrangement suggests advance planning: Jesus has organized the colt's availability as part of a deliberate entry into Jerusalem that enacts Zechariah 9:9 ("your king comes to you, gentle and riding on a donkey"). The entry is not spontaneous but staged as a prophetic action.
4: The colt found "outside in the street, tied at a doorway" — a street-level detail that suggests genuine topographic memory. The location is specific: not inside a courtyard but at the street entrance. The casual precision of this detail (the kind of thing an eyewitness would remember) is consistent with the Petrine tradition that Mark preserves. The disciples' execution of the instruction without incident confirms the prior arrangement.
5: Bystanders ask "What are you doing, untying that colt?" — the challenge is from people present at the scene, not the owners. In Mediterranean village culture, community members monitored public space and would question anyone removing property that did not obviously belong to them. The challenge is not hostile but socially normative: the bystanders are exercising the communal oversight function that village social structure expected. The disciples' response (quoting Jesus's exact instruction) resolves the question immediately.
6: The people "let them go" — the password worked. Whether they were the owners or simply knew of the arrangement, the phrase "the Lord needs it" was sufficient. The smooth execution of the pre-arranged transaction is notable: there is no confusion, no negotiation, no resistance. The colt is released without further incident, confirming the advance arrangement. The narrative efficiency here is deliberate: the focus is on the entrance itself, not on the logistics of the colt.
7: Throwing cloaks over the colt was a practical measure (an unridden colt would not have been saddled) but also a gesture with royal resonance. 2 Kings 9:13 records that when Jehu was acclaimed king, his followers spread their garments under his feet as a gesture of submission and homage. The disciples' action reenacts this royal installation gesture — they are participating in an enthronement procession, with the colt as the royal mount and their cloaks as the royal carpet.
8: Cloaks spread on the road and branches cut from the fields — the collective response of a Passover crowd to a figure they are treating as a royal arrival. Palm branches (specifically named in John 12:13) were a symbol of Judean independence and victory, used at the Maccabean rededication of the temple (1 Macc 13:51; 2 Macc 10:7). Their use during the Passover pilgrim procession carried both political (independence from Rome) and liturgical (festival celebration) connotations. Mark does not specify palms but "branches cut from the fields" — whatever was available from the surrounding vegetation.
9: "Hosanna!" is a transliteration of the Hebrew hoshi'ah-na, "save now/please," from Psalm 118:25. The Hallel Psalms (113-118) were sung during Passover; Psalm 118 was specifically the pilgrim psalm for those arriving at Jerusalem from outside. "Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD" (v.26 of Ps 118) was the formula used to welcome incoming pilgrims — the Levites would chant it from the gates as the procession arrived. The crowd is using standard Passover liturgy, but applying it to Jesus as the specific one who comes.
10: "Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David! Hosanna in the highest heaven!" — these additions to Psalm 118:26 are explicitly Messianic: the crowd is not merely greeting a pilgrim but identifying the moment as the arrival of the Davidic kingdom. "Our father David" is the expected ancestor of the Messiah; "the coming kingdom" is the eschatological expectation. The shout "Hosanna in the highest" redirects the plea from the crowd to heaven — calling on God to save from the highest place. The combination is an unmistakable Messianic demonstration in a city full of pilgrims for Passover.
11: The reconnaissance visit: Jesus enters Jerusalem, enters the temple courts, and surveys everything (periblepsámenos — "looked around at everything," the word of deliberate comprehensive inspection). The late hour means it is too late to act; he withdraws with the Twelve to Bethany for the night. The survey is purposeful: Jesus is planning. The withdrawal to Bethany (where he stayed throughout Passover week; Josephus notes that the surrounding villages were included in the Passover zone) suggests a base of operations outside the city where he could rest and prepare.
12: The fig tree episode (11:12-14 and 11:20-25) brackets the temple cleansing in one of Mark's characteristic intercalations. Near Bethany, walking back toward Jerusalem the next morning, Jesus is hungry. Figs in Palestine ripen fully in June; at Passover (late March/early April) trees would have new leaves but no ripe fruit. However, the first green figs (paggim in Hebrew) that accompany the early leaves were edible even before full ripeness. Their absence means this tree was not producing even preliminary fruit — it had the appearance of fruitfulness (leaves) without substance.
13: Mark's explanatory note — "because it was not the season for figs" — is often cited as a textual difficulty: why curse a tree for lacking figs out of season? The note may be from Mark explaining to a non-Palestinian Gentile audience why an early fig was not expected. Alternatively, the note emphasizes the irony: even out of season, a fig tree with leaves and no figs is an image of false advertising — the appearance of life without its reality. In OT prophecy, the unfruitful fig tree is a consistent image for Israel's failure to produce the covenant faithfulness that its formal religious practices promised (Jer 8:13, Hos 9:10, Mic 7:1).
14: "May no one ever eat fruit from you again" — the curse is a prophetic acted parable, intelligible only in retrospect (when the tree is found withered the next day, 11:20). The disciples "heard him say it" — a mark of eyewitness report. The curse is pronounced on the road to the temple; the temple action follows immediately (vv.15-19); the withering is discovered on the return (vv.20-25). The structural meaning of the intercalation: the temple institution (like the fig tree) bears the appearance of covenant life (sacrifices, prayers, commerce supporting worship) without the fruit God requires.
16: "He would not allow anyone to carry merchandise through the temple courts" — a detail unique to Mark, absent from Matthew and Luke. The Court of the Gentiles (the outermost precinct) was being used as a commercial shortcut between the city and the eastern areas (the Kidron valley, the road to Bethany). Carrying goods through the sacred precinct treated it as a marketplace thoroughfare rather than a house of prayer. Jesus's prohibition of this transit use is a demand that the space be treated as holy — not as a financial convenience. The detail suggests an eyewitness memory of the specific topography.
17: "My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations" — the citation is from Isaiah 56:7, which in its original context is one of the most universalist passages in the OT: foreigners who hold fast to God's covenant will be welcomed to his holy mountain, their sacrifices accepted. The "for all nations" clause (present in Mark, absent from Matthew's citation of the same verse) emphasizes the inclusion of Gentiles as the violated intent of the temple. "Den of robbers" is from Jeremiah 7:11 (the "temple sermon"), where Jeremiah accused Israel of hiding behind the temple while practicing injustice — treating the temple as a magical guarantee of security regardless of conduct.
18: The chief priests and teachers of the law seek to kill Jesus — the official response is immediate and decisive. Their motive is specified: "they feared him, because the whole crowd was amazed at his teaching." The mechanism is social-political: they cannot act against him publicly without triggering a riot, because he has too much popular support. The word for "feared" (ephobēsan) carries both the sense of political apprehension and deeper dread. The temple authorities are caught between their desire to eliminate him and the crowd that makes elimination impossible in this setting.
19: The pattern of leaving the city at evening and returning in the morning (cf. 11:11-12, 11:19-20) reflects both the practical reality of Passover Jerusalem (the city was overcrowded with pilgrims; the Mishnah records regulations about accommodating the increased population, and Bethany was within the halachically defined "greater Jerusalem" for Passover purposes) and Jesus's deliberate management of the situation: presence in the city during the day for teaching and confrontation; withdrawal to the Mount of Olives area at night for safety and rest.
20: On the morning walk from Bethany to Jerusalem, the disciples see the fig tree "withered from the roots" (exērantai ek rizōn). The completeness of the withering — from the roots, not just the branches — confirms that this is not natural die-back but a total and immediate death. The disciples' recognition of the specific tree they passed yesterday ("look, the fig tree you cursed!") establishes continuity between the two scenes. The intercalation is now complete: the temple has been cleansed and the fig tree has died; the two actions illuminate each other.
21: "Rabbi, look! The fig tree you cursed has withered!" — Peter remembers the curse (v.14: "his disciples heard him say it") and makes the connection. The address "Rabbi" (rhabbi) is the standard respectful address for a teacher; in Mark it appears on Peter's lips at the Transfiguration (9:5) and here. The disciples' astonishment at the rapid withering frames the teaching that follows: Jesus uses their surprise as an entry point into a teaching on faith, prayer, and forgiveness that connects to the authority demonstration just completed in the temple.
22: "Have faith in God" (echete pistin theou) — the instruction following the withered fig tree is about trust in God's power to accomplish what appears impossible. The fig tree was cursed by word; it withered. The teaching unpacks what this means for the disciples' own capacity to speak and pray with expectation. The instruction is not about commanding nature arbitrarily but about living in confident dependence on God's word having the power to accomplish what it declares — which is what Jesus has just demonstrated.
23: The mountain-moving saying is a Jewish idiom for accomplishing something extremely difficult. The rabbis called an exceptional teacher who could resolve apparently impossible halakic problems a "rooter up of mountains" or "mountain remover" — a figure of speech for overcoming what seemed insuperable obstacles. Jesus radicalizes the idiom: not just as a description of remarkable teaching ability but as a description of what trusting prayer actually does. The specific image — "say to this mountain, Go, throw yourself into the sea" — may have had topographical resonance on the Mount of Olives overlooking the Dead Sea, though it is primarily proverbial.
24: "Whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours" — the grammar of "believe that you have received it" (aorist, already accomplished) describes trust that anticipates completion. Jewish prayer at this period was typically formulaic (the Amidah, the Eighteen Benedictions) but also included personal petition. The teaching does not provide a technique for prayer but describes the posture of trust from which effective prayer flows: not trying to leverage God into compliance, but speaking from the foundation of confidence in what God has said he will do.
25: "When you stand praying, if you hold anything against anyone, forgive them" — standing was the standard Jewish prayer posture (not kneeling or prostration; cf. Luke 18:11-13; 1 Sam 1:26). The connection between prayer and forgiveness appears in the Lord's Prayer tradition (Matt 6:12,14-15) but here without the Lord's Prayer itself, as a freestanding instruction. The logic is the same: the person who holds unforgiveness is asking for God's mercy while refusing to extend mercy — a structural contradiction that blocks the prayer before it begins.
26: This verse is absent from the earliest and most reliable manuscripts of Mark (Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus, and several other early witnesses) and is widely recognized by textual scholars as a scribal harmonization with Matthew 6:15 ("But if you do not forgive others their sins, your Father will not forgive your sins"). The verse was inserted by copyists to complete the thought from v.25 in explicit parallel with Matthew. Though present in later manuscripts and the KJV tradition, it was almost certainly not part of Mark's original text.
27: They arrive again in Jerusalem — the third consecutive morning return to the city (11:11, 12, 27). "While Jesus was walking in the temple courts" — public, deliberate, visible presence in the space he had cleansed two days earlier. The delegation that approaches him represents the supreme judicial authority: chief priests, scribes, and elders together constitute the Sanhedrin (the full council of 71 that governed Jewish religious and civil life in Jerusalem under Roman oversight). This is not an informal challenge but a formal institutional interrogation of his authority.
28: "By what authority are you doing these things?" — "these things" refers to the entry into Jerusalem, the temple cleansing, and the teaching that followed. The Sanhedrin has authority to examine prophets, teachers, and would-be Messiahs; the question is legitimate. In Jewish legal understanding, authority to act in a religious capacity required either official appointment (priestly ordination, rabbinic ordination) or prophetic commission from God. Jesus has no official appointment; the only alternative is divine commission. The question is: which does he claim, and can he prove it?
29: "I will ask you one question. Answer me, and I will tell you by what authority I am doing these things" — the counter-question strategy is a recognized rabbinic technique (teshuvat ha-sheilah, answering a question with a question). It was considered legitimate as a teaching device, especially when the questioner has not earned the right to a direct answer. Jesus's counter-question about John is not an evasion: it is a test of whether the council can adjudicate a clear case of divine versus human authority. If they cannot evaluate John, they have no standing to evaluate Jesus.
30: "John's baptism — was it from heaven or of human origin?" — the question is perfectly calibrated. John's baptism was a public, known phenomenon; its reception by the entire population of Judea and Jerusalem (1:5) was a matter of record. The question of its origin is exactly parallel to the question they asked about Jesus: was it divinely authorized or merely human initiative? The council must evaluate a closely analogous case before proceeding with Jesus's case. Their answer will reveal whether they have the capacity — and the willingness — to evaluate genuine divine authority at all.
31: The council's private deliberation reveals a group reasoning from consequences rather than from truth: "If we say from heaven, he will ask why we didn't believe him." This is not a bad-faith evasion but an accurate description of their situation — they did not respond to John with belief (cf. Matt 21:32; Luke 7:30) and they know it. The trap is of their own making: they cannot acknowledge John's divine authority without condemning their own prior failure to respond to him, and they cannot deny it without alienating the people.
32: "If we say 'of human origin'..." — the sentence trails off, unfinished in the text, because the problem is obvious: everyone held John to be a genuine prophet, and denying his divine commission would put the council against the entire Jewish public. John's death had made him a popular martyr; challenging his prophetic status in public would be politically suicidal. The council is caught between institutional self-protection (they cannot admit they were wrong about John) and popular pressure (they cannot deny what everyone knows). Both options destroy them.
33: "We don't know" — a formal pleading of ignorance in a legal setting. By admitting they cannot evaluate John's authority, the council surrenders the ground from which they could evaluate Jesus's authority. Jesus's response — "neither will I tell you by what authority I am doing these things" — is not evasion but a formal matching: they have withheld their judgment on a clear case; he withholds his answer to their question. The exchange ends in a public draw, but the moral weight is entirely on Jesus's side: they know the answer but will not say it; he refuses to justify himself before a court that has demonstrated bad faith.
